frameworks.name: neural adaptation
frameworks.one_line: Recent stimulus history reshapes encoding gain and noise correlations in MT.

frameworks.name: population decoding
frameworks.one_line: Single-trial decoding links adapted population activity to perceptual bias.

frameworks.name: direction aftereffect
frameworks.one_line: Repulsive perceptual bias predicted from adaptation-induced population shifts.

core_question: How does brief, continuous adaptation reshape MT population codes to produce persistent perceptual biases for visual motion?
key_assumptions: Encoding changes that go uncorrected by the decoder produce perceptual bias
Simultaneously recorded MT neurons approximate the population code for motion direction
Direction selectivity organizes adaptation effects across the population
manipulations: Direction of the adapting stimulus relative to each neuron's preferred direction
Sequence of motion directions changing every 500 ms
Time elapsed since the adapting stimulus
key_findings: 0.5 s of adaptation altered encoding for up to 2 s
Response gain was reduced for neurons preferring directions near the adapter
Spike-count correlations dropped between pairs tuned near the adapting direction
Single-trial decoding predicted repulsive direction biases
interpretations: Brief adaptation induces persistent, structured changes in MT population codes
These population-level shifts account for the direction aftereffect
